front: What is the primary purpose of the Ljung-Box test in time series analysis?
back: The Ljung-Box test is used to test whether the residuals of a fitted model are serially uncorrelated (i.e., white noise). It checks for the absence of autocorrelation at multiple lags jointly.

front: Define the concept of 'stationarity' in the context of time series.
back: Stationarity refers to a time series whose statistical properties (like mean, variance, and autocorrelation) are constant over time. Most time series models require the data to be stationary to make valid inferences.

front: What is the difference between an ACF (Autocorrelation Function) and a PACF (Partial Autocorrelation Function) plot?
back: The ACF measures the correlation between observations at different lags, including all intermediate lags. The PACF measures the correlation between observations at two lags, but with the linear effect of the intermediate lags removed, isolating the direct relationship.

front: What is the main goal of performing a 'unit root test' like the Dickey-Fuller test?
back: A unit root test determines whether a time series is non-stationary due to the presence of a stochastic trend. It tests the null hypothesis that a unit root is present (non-stationary) against the alternative hypothesis of stationarity.

front: What does an ARIMA(p,d,q) model represent?
back: ARIMA stands for Autoregressive Integrated Moving Average. The 'p' is the order of the autoregressive (AR) part, 'd' is the degree of differencing needed to make the series stationary, and 'q' is the order of the moving average (MA) part.
